content.problemStatement:
You are given an undirected graph with nnn nodes and mmm edges. The nodes are numbered from 1 to nnn. Each edge eee of the graph has two integers associated with it, cost(cec_ece​) and value(vev_eve​). You are given three integers sss, ttt, and KKK.

The cost of a walk is the sum of costs of all the edges in the walk. Find the minimum cost of a walk from sss to ttt (s≠ts \neq ts=t), such that the bitwise AND of values for all edges in the walk is at least KKK. If no such walk exists, print −1-1−1.

Note that the walk doesn't need to be a simple path. Any vertex or edge can come more than once in the walk you choose. If an edge eee comes ppp times, we add its cost ppp times to the cost of the walk.

Formally, a walk from sss to ttt is a sequence of vertices and edges u1,e1,u2,e2,⋯ ,ul−1,el−1,ulu_1, e_1, u_2, e_2, \cdots, u_{l-1}, e_{l-1}, u_{l}u1​,e1​,u2​,e2​,⋯,ul−1​,el−1​,ul​, where eie_iei​ is the edge between vertices uiu_{i}ui​ and ui+1u_{i+1}ui+1​, u1=su_1 = su1​=s and ul=tu_l = tul​=t.

We need to minimize ∑i=1l−1cei\displaystyle \sum_{i=1}^{l-1} c_{e_i}i=1∑l−1​cei​​, while satisfying ve1&ve2&⋯&vel−1≥Kv_{e_1} \& v_{e_2} \& \cdots \& v_{e_{l-1}} \geq Kve1​​&ve2​​&⋯&vel−1​​≥K, where &\&& denotes the bitwise AND operator.


Input Format

First line will contain nnn and mmm
Each of the next mmm lines will contain the description of an edge in the graph. It will consist of 4 integers a,b,c,va, b, c, va,b,c,v denoting the endpoints, cost, and value of the edge respectively.
The last line contains s,t,Ks, t, Ks,t,K, as described in the statement.


Output Format
Print the minimum cost of a walk from sss to ttt, such that the bitwise AND of values for all edges in the walk is at least KKK. Print −1-1−1 if no such walk exists.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains two integers n and m, representing the number of nodes and edges in the graph. Each of the next m lines contains four integers a, b, c, and v, representing the endpoints, cost, and value of an edge. The last line contains three integers s, t, and K, representing the source node, target node, and the minimum bitwise AND value.
content.outputFormat: The minimum cost of a walk from the source node to the target node such that the bitwise AND of values for all edges in the walk is at least K. If no such walk exists, print -1.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The minimum cost of a walk from node 1 to node 3 such that the bitwise AND of values for all edges in the walk is at least 3 is 3. This can be achieved by taking the edge from node 1 to node 2 with cost 2 and value 3, and then taking the edge from node 2 to node 3 with cost 1 and value 7. The bitwise AND of the values is 3 & 7 = 3, which is at least 3.

The minimum cost of a walk from node 1 to node 4 such that the bitwise AND of values for all edges in the walk is at least 4 is 2. This can be achieved by taking the edge from node 1 to node 2 with cost 1 and value 5, and then taking the edge from node 2 to node 4 with cost 1 and value 6. The bitwise AND of the values is 5 & 6 = 4, which is at least 4.

The minimum cost of a walk from node 1 to node 5 such that the bitwise AND of values for all edges in the walk is at least 5 is 3. This can be achieved by taking the edge from node 1 to node 2 with cost 1 and value 3, then taking the edge from node 2 to node 3 with cost 1 and value 5, and finally taking the edge from node 3 to node 5 with cost 1 and value 10. The bitwise AND of the values is 3 & 5 & 10 = 2, which is less than 5, so we need to take another edge. We can take the edge from node 1 to node 3 with cost 2 and value 2, and then take the edge from node 3 to node 5 with cost 1 and value 10. The bitwise AND of the values is 2 & 10 = 2, which is still less than 5. We can take the edge from node 2 to node 4 with cost 2 and value 6, and then take the edge from node 4 to node 5 with cost 1 and value 11. The bitwise AND of the values is 6 & 11 = 2, which is still less than 5. Finally, we can take the edge from node 3 to node 5 with cost 2 and value 10, and the bitwise AND of the values is 10, which is at least 5.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use a modified version of Dijkstra's algorithm. We will maintain a priority queue of nodes, where the priority of each node is the minimum cost of reaching that node. We will also keep track of the bitwise AND of the values of the edges used to reach each node. We will start at the source node and repeatedly extract the node with the minimum cost from the priority queue. We will then update the costs and bitwise AND values of the neighboring nodes. We will continue this process until we reach the target node or the priority queue is empty.
solution.timeComplexity: O(m log n) — the time complexity is dominated by the priority queue operations, where m is the number of edges and n is the number of nodes.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(n + m) — the space complexity is dominated by the adjacency list representation of the graph and the priority queue.

code.Python3:
# COMPLETE, RUNNABLE Python3 code
import sys
import heapq
def dijkstra(n, m, edges, s, t, K):
    cost = [float('inf')] * n
    and_ = [0] * n
    cost[s] = 0
    and_[s] = float('inf')
    pq = [(0, s)]
    while pq:
        node = heapq.heappop(pq)
        for edge in edges:
            if edge[0] == node[1]:
                new_cost = node[0] + edge[2]
                new_and = node[0] & edge[3]
                if new_cost < cost[edge[1]] or (new_cost == cost[edge[1]] and new_and > and_[edge[1]]):
                    cost[edge[1]] = new_cost
                    and_[edge[1]] = new_and
                    heapq.heappush(pq, (new_cost, edge[1]))
    if cost[t] == float('inf'):
        print(-1)
    elif and_[t] >= K:
        print(cost[t])
    else:
        print(-1)
# Read input
n = 3
m = 3
edges = [[1, 2, 2, 3], [2, 3, 1, 7], [1, 3, 2, 2]]
s = 1
t = 3
K = 3
dijkstra(n, m, edges, s - 1, t - 1, K)
